rugosa</italic> aerial parts were separated and identified by gas chromatography-flame ionization detection and gas chromatography-mass spectrometry using an Agilent 6890N gas chromatography system connected to an Agilent 5973N mass selective detector.

Fractions 5–8, 10, 12 that possessed nematicidal toxicity, and with similar TLC profiles, were pooled and further purified by preparative silica gel column chromatography with petroleum ether-acetone until to obtain the pure compounds for determining their structures based on nuclear magnetic resonance spectroscopy.

Results from all replicates for the pure compounds and essential oil were subjected to probit analysis using the PriProbit Program V1.6.3 to determine LC50 values and their 95% confidence intervals .

Repeat Sequence Analysis

Simple sequence repeats in Aletris medogensis and the 13 published Aletris accessions were identified using MISA software (Beier et al.

Then, long sequence repeats , including forward , reverse , palindrome , and complementary repeats were detected within the LSC, IRa/b, and SSC regions of the <italic toggle="yes">Aletris</italic> chloroplast genomes using the REPuter program (Kurtz et al.

The GC contents of the Aletris cp genomes were similar , with the IRs showing the highest GC levels , followed by the LSC and SSC regions .

The cp genomes of Aletris are highly similar in terms of gene content, with most encoding 131 genes, including 85 protein‐coding genes , 38 tRNA genes, and 8 rRNA genes .

REPuter identified 34–45 repeat sequences with a length > 30 bp, of which palindromic and forward repeat sequences were most abundant, while complement and reverse repeat sequences were very rare and almost nonexistent in many species .

Variance Analysis of the <styled-content style="fixed-case" toggle="no">IR</styled-content> Boundaries

We compared the IR boundaries among 17 Nartheciaceae cp genomes, including Dioscorea futschauensis, Metanarthecium luteoviride, Narthecium ossifragum, and the 14 Aletris accessions mentioned above , observing minor variations in gene content, structure, and order at the quadripartite borders .

In addition, an inversion event involving the ndhF and ycf1 genes in the Dioscorea futschauensis cp genome occurred, with the former now extending 6 bp into the JSA, and the latter was cut through by the JSB boundary.

Comparison of LSC, SSC, and IR region boundaries in 17 Nartheciaceae chloroplast genomes.

Candidate Molecular Markers Identification

Using sliding window analysis, 18 hypervariable regions were identified as potential East Asia‐specific molecular markers for Aletris taxonomy, primarily occurring in the LSC and SSC regions and ranging from 523 to 2133 bp .

We found that the relationships among all 14 East Asian Aletris species were completely congruent between ML and BI analyses, forming a strongly supported monophyletic group that was sister to the outgroup taxa: Dioscorea futschauensis, Metanarthecium luteoviride, and Narthecium ossifragum .

Therefore, the employment of nuclear markers, or a combination of nuclear and plastid data, is strongly advocated in future studies to further validate and refine the phylogenetic relationships proposed here.

Hypervariable Regions and Molecular Markers

The hypervariable regions identified here , including ycf1 , petA , and rpoC2 gene regions, offer superior resolution for species delimitation compared to traditional markers (Zhao et al.

Additionally, the genomic resources generated here, including hypervariable markers and cp genome annotations, will facilitate monitoring efforts and inform conservation strategies for this endemic lineage.

Conclusions

The integration of morphology, chloroplast genomics, and phylogenomics effectively resolves taxonomic conflicts in Aletris and reveals evolutionary mechanisms underlying species diversification.

The natural products obtained from medicinal plants, including flavonoids, polyphenols, terpenoids , saponins , alkaloids , and mucilaginous polysaccharides , show immense pharmacological significance such as anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, antiulcer, anti-oxidant, and anticancer activities .

The anti-ulcer properties of plants may be attributed to the flavonoid and triterpene contents .

Anti-oxidants can scavenge free radicals before attacking cells and biological targets .

Therefore, a considerable interest is critical to find natural anti-oxidants from herbal origins to replace synthetic ones for maintaining optimal protection against many disorders, such as gastric ulcers .

Polyphenols have been associated with anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, and immunomodulatory properties.
Additionally, flavonoids maintain the gastric mucosal glycoprotein moiety, and may cause an increase in nitric oxide action .

Saponins act mainly through anti-secretory mechanisms as they inhibit acid secretion and total acid output and lower the pH value of gastric juice .

Triterpenoids have several pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory and anti-ulcer activities .

Additionally, they accelerate ulcer healing by anti-oxidant activity through reduction of lipid peroxide content and elevation of SOD activity in the stomach .

Alkaloids have a gastro-protective activity, which may involve the participation of sulfhydryl compounds, NO and PG, as well as reduction of IL-1β and TNF-α contents and elevation of GSH contents .

Most of the reported alkaloids possess prophylactic anti-ulcerogenic activity through enhancing mucus production in addition to the anti-oxidant activity .

Overview of Methodology

To fulfill the purpose of the present investigation, a preliminary phytochemical study of the prepared extracts used in this study had to be done followed by evaluation of the in vitro anti-oxidant activity of the extracts .

This was accompanied by assessment of aggressive factors like peptic activity, histamine and gastrin contents, and measurement of oxidative stress biomarkers including superoxide-dismutase activity and reduced glutathione , nitric oxide , and malondialdehyde contents and protective factors including prostaglandin-E<sub>2</sub> and prostacyclin contents in accordance with assessment of immunological factors including tumor-necrosis-factor-alpha , interleukin-1-beta , cystathionine-β-synthase , and heme-oxygenase-1 expressions.

Preliminary Phytochemical Screening of Extracts

The extracts of Althaea officinalis and Solanum nigrum were subjected to preliminary phytochemical screening .

These methods are conducted to detect the presence or absence of carbohydrates by the Molisch test, tannins by the FeCl3 test, flavonoids by the NaOH/HCl test, saponins by the froth test, and for glycosides: anthraquinon glycosides by the Born-trager test, cardiac glycosides by the Keller-Killiani test and cyanogenic glycosides by the sodium picrate paper test, sterols and triterpenoids by the Salkowski test, alkaloids by the Dragendorff test, and volatile oils by the Sudan III test and by checking if the extract had a characteristic odor or not.

Discussion

In the present study, the gastro-protective activity and the anti-oxidant effect of Althaea officinalis and Solanum nigrum extracts was investigated in pyloric-ligation/indomethacin-induced gastric-ulceration in male rats and their possible underlying mechanisms included in their actions were investigated.

Preliminary phytochemical analysis of test extracts was accomplished prior to the pharmacological study.

<italic toggle="yes">Solanum nigrum</italic> fruit extract was positive for carbohydrates, tannins, flavonoids, saponins, sterols and triterpenoids and alkaloids, in agreement with the results obtained by El-Hawary et al.

Misoprostol protects the GI mucosa by stimulating mucus/bicarbonate secretion .

Some herbs have shown inhibition of gastric mucosal damage induced experimentally by necrotizing agents through their anti-secretory and anti-oxidant properties .

Althaea officinalis is traditionally used as a treatment for the irritation of mucous membranes, including use as a gargle for mouth and throat ulcers and gastric ulcers .

The observed gastro-protective effect of <italic toggle="yes">Althaea officinalis</italic> could be attributed to active phytoconstituents found in the extract such as flavonoids and mucilage polysaccharides .

The observed cytoprotective and anti-oxidative activity of Solanum nigrum extract is attributed to the presence of biologically active phytoconstituents having anti-oxidative nature .

Results of the current investigation revealed that pyloric ligation of rat stomachs followed by administration of indomethacin caused significant ulceration in the glandular area and degenerative changes in gastric tissues of the rat stomach as seen in histopathological examination as it showed epithelial loss, congested blood vessels with inflammatory cell infiltration, associated with considerable elevation in ulcer number and ulcer index, which was in agreement with previous studies that indicated that NSAIDs like indomethacin can generate observable gastric ulcers in experimental animals .

Pyloric-ligation/indomethacin-induced mucosal injury is attributed to various processes, including infiltration of leukocytes, inhibition of PG-E2, initiation of lipid peroxidation, decreasing levels of anti-oxidants, and induction of apoptosis .

Pretreatment with omeprazole protected animals from pyloric-ligation/indomethacin-induced gastric-ulceration as manifested by significantly reduced ulcer number and ulcer index.

Whether misoprostol has a direct anti-oxidant activity or the reduction in MDA results indirectly from minimizing the stressful conditions imposed by indomethacin, resulting from inhibition of indomethacin-induced gastric ulcers, needs to be clarified .

Althaea officinalis pretreatment significantly increased NO, similar results that revealed that flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis are anti-inflammatory and useful in maintaining healthy circulation and are protective against gastric ulcers .

Also, it was demonstrated that flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis can scavenge free radicals and can inhibit lipid peroxidation .

Data of the current work also demonstrated that Solanum nigrum extract significantly increased gastric GSH and NO contents and SOD activity but significantly decreased MDA content after pyloric-ligation/indomethacin administration.

In contrast, another study demonstrated that the flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis can improve blood circulation .

In addition, Solanum nigrum extract pretreatment increased PG-E2 significantly and PG-I2 contents in comparison to gastric ulcer control group.

Also, the flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis have anti-inflammatory and antitumor agents, moreover, some of them have antiproliferative and apoptotic effects on cancer cell lines .

Solanum nigrum pretreatment significantly decreased TNF-α and IL-1β contents in comparison to the gastric ulcer control group.

HO-1 and its metabolites have the potential to counteract the NSAIDs-induced gastric injury and show its gastro-protective effects by different mechanisms, including anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory properties and their ability to restore mucosal blood flow .

Gaseous vasoactive mediators, such as hydrogen sulfide , were shown to play an important role in the mechanism of mucosal defense and gastro-protection .

The generation of the gastric ulceration induced by indomethacin takes place by inhibition of protective factors like prostaglandin-E2 , bicarbonate, and mucus, together with potentiation of aggressive factors including increased gastric hyperacidity and oxidative stress .

Proton pump inhibitors , including omeprazole, are frequently used for therapeutic management of acid-related disorders such as gastroesophageal reflux disease and Zollinger–Ellison syndrome and for the management of peptic-ulcer diseases induced by stress, NSAIDs, and Helicobacter pylori infection.

The natural products obtained from medicinal plants, including flavonoids, polyphenols, terpenoids , saponins , alkaloids , and mucilaginous polysaccharides , show immense pharmacological significance such as anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, antiulcer, anti-oxidant, and anticancer activities .

The anti-ulcer properties of plants may be attributed to the flavonoid and triterpene contents .

Anti-oxidants can scavenge free radicals before attacking cells and biological targets .

Therefore, a considerable interest is critical to find natural anti-oxidants from herbal origins to replace synthetic ones for maintaining optimal protection against many disorders, such as gastric ulcers .

Polyphenols have been associated with anti-inflammatory, anti-oxidant, and immunomodulatory properties.
Additionally, flavonoids maintain the gastric mucosal glycoprotein moiety, and may cause an increase in nitric oxide action .

Saponins act mainly through anti-secretory mechanisms as they inhibit acid secretion and total acid output and lower the pH value of gastric juice .

Triterpenoids have several pharmacological activities, including anti-inflammatory and anti-ulcer activities .

Additionally, they accelerate ulcer healing by anti-oxidant activity through reduction of lipid peroxide content and elevation of SOD activity in the stomach .

Alkaloids have a gastro-protective activity, which may involve the participation of sulfhydryl compounds, NO and PG, as well as reduction of IL-1β and TNF-α contents and elevation of GSH contents .

Most of the reported alkaloids possess prophylactic anti-ulcerogenic activity through enhancing mucus production in addition to the anti-oxidant activity .

Althaea officinalis L.

The pharmacological and anti-oxidant activities of Althaea officinalis were attributed to various compounds such as polysaccharides and flavonoids present in the plant .

Solanum nigrum L., family: Solanaceae has been extensively used in traditional medicine.

Overview of Methodology

To fulfill the purpose of the present investigation, a preliminary phytochemical study of the prepared extracts used in this study had to be done followed by evaluation of the in vitro anti-oxidant activity of the extracts .

This was accompanied by assessment of aggressive factors like peptic activity, histamine and gastrin contents, and measurement of oxidative stress biomarkers including superoxide-dismutase activity and reduced glutathione , nitric oxide , and malondialdehyde contents and protective factors including prostaglandin-E<sub>2</sub> and prostacyclin contents in accordance with assessment of immunological factors including tumor-necrosis-factor-alpha , interleukin-1-beta , cystathionine-β-synthase , and heme-oxygenase-1 expressions.

Preliminary Phytochemical Screening of Extracts

The extracts of Althaea officinalis and Solanum nigrum were subjected to preliminary phytochemical screening .

These methods are conducted to detect the presence or absence of carbohydrates by the Molisch test, tannins by the FeCl3 test, flavonoids by the NaOH/HCl test, saponins by the froth test, and for glycosides: anthraquinon glycosides by the Born-trager test, cardiac glycosides by the Keller-Killiani test and cyanogenic glycosides by the sodium picrate paper test, sterols and triterpenoids by the Salkowski test, alkaloids by the Dragendorff test, and volatile oils by the Sudan III test and by checking if the extract had a characteristic odor or not.

2.6.

Preliminary Phytochemical Screening of Extracts

Preliminary phytochemical analysis for Althaea officinalis extract was positive for carbohydrates, traces of tannins, flavonoids, traces of saponins, and volatile oils.

Preliminary phytochemical analysis for Solanum nigrum extract was positive for carbohydrates, tannins, flavonoids, saponins, sterols and triterpenoids and alkaloids .

3.2.

Routine H and E Staining

As shown in Figure 4 and Table 2, normal rats’ stomachs showed normal histological structures of gastric mucosa including glandular tissue, submucosa, muscular coat, and covering serosa without abnormal alterations .

Discussion

In the present study, the gastro-protective activity and the anti-oxidant effect of Althaea officinalis and Solanum nigrum extracts was investigated in pyloric-ligation/indomethacin-induced gastric-ulceration in male rats and their possible underlying mechanisms included in their actions were investigated.

Preliminary phytochemical analysis of test extracts was accomplished prior to the pharmacological study.

<italic toggle="yes">Solanum nigrum</italic> fruit extract was positive for carbohydrates, tannins, flavonoids, saponins, sterols and triterpenoids and alkaloids, in agreement with the results obtained by El-Hawary et al.

Misoprostol protects the GI mucosa by stimulating mucus/bicarbonate secretion .

Some herbs have shown inhibition of gastric mucosal damage induced experimentally by necrotizing agents through their anti-secretory and anti-oxidant properties .

Althaea officinalis is traditionally used as a treatment for the irritation of mucous membranes, including use as a gargle for mouth and throat ulcers and gastric ulcers .

The observed gastro-protective effect of <italic toggle="yes">Althaea officinalis</italic> could be attributed to active phytoconstituents found in the extract such as flavonoids and mucilage polysaccharides .

The observed cytoprotective and anti-oxidative activity of Solanum nigrum extract is attributed to the presence of biologically active phytoconstituents having anti-oxidative nature .

Results of the current investigation revealed that pyloric ligation of rat stomachs followed by administration of indomethacin caused significant ulceration in the glandular area and degenerative changes in gastric tissues of the rat stomach as seen in histopathological examination as it showed epithelial loss, congested blood vessels with inflammatory cell infiltration, associated with considerable elevation in ulcer number and ulcer index, which was in agreement with previous studies that indicated that NSAIDs like indomethacin can generate observable gastric ulcers in experimental animals .

Pyloric-ligation/indomethacin-induced mucosal injury is attributed to various processes, including infiltration of leukocytes, inhibition of PG-E2, initiation of lipid peroxidation, decreasing levels of anti-oxidants, and induction of apoptosis .

Pretreatment with omeprazole protected animals from pyloric-ligation/indomethacin-induced gastric-ulceration as manifested by significantly reduced ulcer number and ulcer index.

Whether misoprostol has a direct anti-oxidant activity or the reduction in MDA results indirectly from minimizing the stressful conditions imposed by indomethacin, resulting from inhibition of indomethacin-induced gastric ulcers, needs to be clarified .

Althaea officinalis pretreatment significantly increased NO, similar results that revealed that flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis are anti-inflammatory and useful in maintaining healthy circulation and are protective against gastric ulcers .

Also, it was demonstrated that flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis can scavenge free radicals and can inhibit lipid peroxidation .

Data of the current work also demonstrated that Solanum nigrum extract significantly increased gastric GSH and NO contents and SOD activity but significantly decreased MDA content after pyloric-ligation/indomethacin administration.

In contrast, another study demonstrated that the flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis can improve blood circulation .

In addition, Solanum nigrum extract pretreatment increased PG-E2 significantly and PG-I2 contents in comparison to gastric ulcer control group.

Also, the flavonoids present in Althaea officinalis have anti-inflammatory and antitumor agents, moreover, some of them have antiproliferative and apoptotic effects on cancer cell lines .

Solanum nigrum pretreatment significantly decreased TNF-α and IL-1β contents in comparison to the gastric ulcer control group.

HO-1 and its metabolites have the potential to counteract the NSAIDs-induced gastric injury and show its gastro-protective effects by different mechanisms, including anti-oxidant and anti-inflammatory properties and their ability to restore mucosal blood flow .

Gaseous vasoactive mediators, such as hydrogen sulfide , were shown to play an important role in the mechanism of mucosal defense and gastro-protection .

rejected.phrase: Comparative analysis of 14 East Asian <italic toggle="no">Aletris</italic> complete chloroplast genomes revealed a conserved quadripartite structure with species‐specific variations, including pseudogenization of <italic toggle="no">ycf1</italic>, loss of <italic toggle="no">rrn4.5</italic>, and shifts in IR boundaries.

We identified 18 hypervariable regions as potential molecular markers and detected signals of positive selection in genes <styled-content style="fixed-case" toggle="no"> <italic toggle="no">ccsA</italic> </styled-content>, <styled-content style="fixed-case" toggle="no"> <italic toggle="no">cemA</italic> </styled-content>, and <italic toggle="no">rps12</italic>, suggesting adaptive evolution.

In addition, the genomic data of other closely related species within Nartheciaceae, including 13 <italic toggle="yes">Aletris</italic>, 1 <italic toggle="yes">Metanarthecium</italic>, 1 Nartheciaceae, and 1 <italic toggle="yes">Dioscorea</italic> accession , were also downloaded and curated.

Specifically, genomes with problematic annotations were systematically re‐annotated using our standardized PGA protocol to ensure data uniformity.

Repeat Sequence Analysis

Simple sequence repeats in Aletris medogensis and the 13 published Aletris accessions were identified using MISA software (Beier et al.

Then, long sequence repeats , including forward , reverse , palindrome , and complementary repeats were detected within the LSC, IRa/b, and SSC regions of the <italic toggle="yes">Aletris</italic> chloroplast genomes using the REPuter program (Kurtz et al.

The GC contents of the Aletris cp genomes were similar , with the IRs showing the highest GC levels , followed by the LSC and SSC regions .

The cp genomes of Aletris are highly similar in terms of gene content, with most encoding 131 genes, including 85 protein‐coding genes , 38 tRNA genes, and 8 rRNA genes .

REPuter identified 34–45 repeat sequences with a length > 30 bp, of which palindromic and forward repeat sequences were most abundant, while complement and reverse repeat sequences were very rare and almost nonexistent in many species .

Number of LSRs at different locations in each genome.

Variance Analysis of the <styled-content style="fixed-case" toggle="no">IR</styled-content> Boundaries

We compared the IR boundaries among 17 Nartheciaceae cp genomes, including Dioscorea futschauensis, Metanarthecium luteoviride, Narthecium ossifragum, and the 14 Aletris accessions mentioned above , observing minor variations in gene content, structure, and order at the quadripartite borders .

In addition, an inversion event involving the ndhF and ycf1 genes in the Dioscorea futschauensis cp genome occurred, with the former now extending 6 bp into the JSA, and the latter was cut through by the JSB boundary.

Comparison of LSC, SSC, and IR region boundaries in 17 Nartheciaceae chloroplast genomes.

Candidate Molecular Markers Identification

Using sliding window analysis, 18 hypervariable regions were identified as potential East Asia‐specific molecular markers for Aletris taxonomy, primarily occurring in the LSC and SSC regions and ranging from 523 to 2133 bp .

The 18 regions with high diversity are indicated above the peaks.

Phylogenetic Relationship of <italic toggle="no">Aletris</italic>

We found that the relationships among all 14 East Asian Aletris species were completely congruent between ML and BI analyses, forming a strongly supported monophyletic group that was sister to the outgroup taxa: Dioscorea futschauensis, Metanarthecium luteoviride, and Narthecium ossifragum .

Therefore, the employment of nuclear markers, or a combination of nuclear and plastid data, is strongly advocated in future studies to further validate and refine the phylogenetic relationships proposed here.

Hypervariable Regions and Molecular Markers

The hypervariable regions identified here , including ycf1 , petA , and rpoC2 gene regions, offer superior resolution for species delimitation compared to traditional markers (Zhao et al.

Additionally, the genomic resources generated here, including hypervariable markers and cp genome annotations, will facilitate monitoring efforts and inform conservation strategies for this endemic lineage.

Conclusions

The integration of morphology, chloroplast genomics, and phylogenomics effectively resolves taxonomic conflicts in Aletris and reveals evolutionary mechanisms underlying species diversification.
